patient.name: Dorothy Whitman
patient.chiefComplaint: Worsening shortness of breath and productive cough over three days

history.hpi: 68-year-old female with severe COPD (GOLD stage 4) on 2L home oxygen at baseline. Her dyspnea has been worsening over three days with a productive green cough. Prescribed a rescue prednisone pack by her primary care doctor yesterday but has only taken one dose. Called EMS today because she can no longer walk to the bathroom without stopping to catch her breath.
history.events: Three days of worsening productive cough and dyspnea. Prednisone taper started yesterday. Called EMS when unable to ambulate to bathroom.

scene.dispatch: Medic 6, respond code 2 to 1901 Riverside Terrace apartment 14, for a 68-year-old female with shortness of breath, known COPD.
scene.location: First-floor senior apartment. Patient in a recliner in the living room, home oxygen concentrator running with nasal cannula at 2 LPM.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Note O2 concentrator and avoid open flame (no-one smoking).
scene.bystanders: Patient's daughter is present, provides medication list, confirms three-day worsening
visualCues: Elderly thin female seated in a recliner, leaning forward in tripod posture
Visible accessory muscle use including strap muscles of the neck
Home oxygen nasal cannula at 2 LPM (her baseline)
Small amount of thick green sputum in a tissue
Pulse oximeter on the side table reading 84%

phases.name: Arrival — COPD Exacerbation with Borderline Respiratory Failure
phases.description: Classic severe COPD exacerbation. Important nuance: oxygen must be titrated — a chronic COPD patient retains CO2 and depends partly on hypoxic drive. Over-oxygenation can precipitate CO2 narcosis. Target saturation is 88-92%, not 94-98%. CPAP/BiPAP with in-line bronchodilators is the mainstay.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Tachypneic at 30/min with diffuse wheezes and prolonged expiratory phase; accessory muscle use; speaking in 3-4 word phrases
otherFindings: Pursed-lip breathing — a learned compensatory technique
Diffuse expiratory wheezes with prolonged expiratory phase
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'Just... so tired... of breathing' (3-4 words at a time)

expectedActions.action: Leave patient in position of comfort (tripod)
Titrate supplemental oxygen to target SpO2 88-92% (permissive hypoxia is appropriate in chronic COPD); avoid over-oxygenation which can worsen hypercapnia
expectedActions.rationale: Chronic CO2 retainers can develop worsening hypercapnia with high FiO2 via multiple mechanisms (Haldane effect, V/Q mismatch, blunted hypoxic drive). Target 88-92%.

expectedActions.action: Administer Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized; repeat albuterol q20 min or continuous as needed
Administer Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg IV (37 mg, max 16 mg — so give 16 mg) IV or PO; or Methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg IV (125 mg)
Apply CPAP or BiPAP starting at 5 cmH2O with in-line albuterol; coach the patient through mask application
expectedActions.rationale: NIV reduces work of breathing, improves CO2 clearance, and prevents intubation — the most important intervention in severe COPD exacerbation.

expectedActions.action: Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography (watch for shark fin and trending CO2)
Monitor mental status continuously — decline in consciousness suggests CO2 narcosis and is an indication for escalation to BiPAP or intubation

phases.name: Responding to NIV and Bronchodilators
phases.description: With CPAP and aggressive nebulizers, the patient's work of breathing reduces noticeably. SpO2 holds at 90%, EtCO2 trending down, mental status intact. Continue therapy and transport to the ED for admission.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: CPAP in place, respiratory rate decreasing, pursed-lip breathing less pronounced, expiratory wheezes improving

expectedActions.action: Continue back-to-back albuterol nebulizer via CPAP mask
Do not break the CPAP seal until ED bedside with replacement ready
Continuous mental status reassessment — watch for somnolence (CO2 narcosis)
Transport to ED with notification; patient will likely need admission for COPD exacerbation

phases.name: Branch: CO2 Narcosis from Over-Oxygenation
phases.description: Crew applies high-flow oxygen at 15 LPM via NRB without titration. Over the next 10 minutes, the patient becomes progressively somnolent as CO2 climbs, eventually losing consciousness. This teaches the lesson of oxygen titration in chronic CO2 retainers.
phases.triggerCondition: High-flow O2 without titration leading to somnolence

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Slowing respiratory rate, shallow, not improving with NRB alone
otherFindings: Patient is pink and warm but confused
Decreased respiratory effort despite ongoing distress

expectedActions.action: Recognize CO2 narcosis — a pink, warm, confused patient with SpO2 98% is not reassuring in COPD
Escalate to BiPAP (inspiratory and expiratory pressures) which actively supports CO2 clearance; titrate O2 down to target SpO2 88-92%
If mental status continues to decline, prepare for RSI — BiPAP is only appropriate if the patient can cooperate

criticalActions: Titrate oxygen to target SpO2 88-92% in chronic COPD (not 94-98%)
Administer Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized with repetition
Administer IV steroids (Dexamethasone 16 mg OR Methylprednisolone 125 mg)
Apply CPAP or BiPAP with in-line bronchodilators
Coach the patient through mask application
Continuous mental status monitoring for CO2 narcosis
Do not discontinue NIV until ED bedside
expectedActions: Keep patient in position of comfort
bonusActions: Recognize that SpO2 98% in a chronic retainer is NOT reassuring
Articulate the physiology of CO2 narcosis
Consider BiPAP (rather than CPAP) specifically for CO2 retention

learningObjectives: Understand oxygen titration in chronic CO2 retainers (target 88-92%)
Apply NIV as first-line therapy in severe COPD exacerbation
Recognize CO2 narcosis as an indication for escalation
Apply MATC Respiratory Distress protocol for COPD
discussionQuestions: Why can high-flow oxygen worsen hypercapnia in COPD?
How do CPAP and BiPAP differ, and which is preferred in hypercapnia?
What is the target SpO2 in chronic COPD and why is it different from other patients?
What are the signs of CO2 narcosis?
When would intubation be appropriate in a COPD exacerbation?
commonPitfalls: Interpreting SpO2 98% in a retainer as improvement
Breaking CPAP seal during transfer to ED
keyTakeaways: Titrate oxygen — SpO2 88-92% is the goal
NIV is the most impactful intervention in COPD exacerbation
Mental status change = CO2 narcosis until proven otherwise
BiPAP is preferred over CPAP when hypercapnia is the driver
